questions.question: Define a subgroup and prove that the intersection of two subgroups of G is also a subgroup of G.
keyPoints: Definition of subgroup with three conditions
Take any two subgroups H₁ and H₂ of G
Show intersection is non-empty (contains identity)
Show closure: if a,b ∈ H₁∩H₂, then ab ∈ H₁∩H₂
Show inverses: if a ∈ H₁∩H₂, then a⁻¹ ∈ H₁∩H₂

questions.question: State and prove the two-step subgroup test.
keyPoints: State the two conditions: closure and inverses
Prove necessity: if H is subgroup, then closure holds and inverses exist
Prove sufficiency: show identity exists in H
Show a*b = a*(b⁻¹)⁻¹ to prove closure from inverse property
Conclude H is a subgroup

questions.question: Define cyclic subgroup generated by an element a. Find all elements of the cyclic subgroup generated by 4 in Z₁₂.
keyPoints: Define ⟨a⟩ = {aⁿ : n ∈ Z}
In Z₁₂, compute powers of 4 under addition
4 + 4 = 8, 8 + 4 = 12 ≡ 0, then repeats
List elements: {0, 4, 8}
State order of the cyclic subgroup is 3

questions.question: State Lagrange's Theorem and hence show that any group of prime order has no non-trivial proper subgroups.
keyPoints: State Lagrange's Theorem: |H| divides |G| for finite G
Let |G| = p (prime)
By Lagrange, any subgroup H has order 1 or p
Order 1 gives trivial subgroup, order p gives G itself
Conclude no proper subgroups exist

questions.question: Define the center Z(G) of a group G. Show that Z(G) is a normal subgroup of G.
keyPoints: Define Z(G) = {z ∈ G : zg = gz for all g ∈ G}
Show Z(G) is a subgroup: contains identity, closed, has inverses
For normality: need to show gZ(G) = Z(G)g for all g
For any z ∈ Z(G), gzg⁻¹ = zgg⁻¹ = z ∈ Z(G)
Conclude gZ(G)g⁻¹ ⊆ Z(G), hence normal

questions.question: Define left coset and right coset of a subgroup H in G. When are they equal?
keyPoints: Define left coset: gH = {gh : h ∈ H}
Define right coset: Hg = {hg : h ∈ H}
They are equal when H is a normal subgroup
Equivalent condition: gH = Hg for all g ∈ G
Or equivalently, gHg⁻¹ = H for all g ∈ G

questions.question: Apply the finite subgroup test to show that {0, 3, 6, 9} is a subgroup of Z₁₂.
keyPoints: State finite subgroup test
The set is non-empty and finite
Check closure: add any two elements mod 12
All sums remain in the set, hence it's a subgroup

questions.question: Let G be a finite group and H a subgroup. Prove that the number of left cosets of H in G equals the number of right cosets of H in G.
keyPoints: Define the map φ: gH → Hg⁻¹
Show φ is well-defined: if g₁H = g₂H then g₁⁻¹ = g₂⁻¹h for some h∈H
Prove φ is one-to-one
Prove φ is onto
Conclude |{gH}| = |{Hg}|

questions.question: (a) Define order of an element and order of a group. (b) Prove that every cyclic group is abelian. (c) Find all generators of the cyclic group Z₈.
keyPoints: (a) Order of element a is smallest n>0 with aⁿ=e; order of group is |G|
(b) For cyclic group G=⟨a⟩, take any xy ∈ G, write as aⁿaᵐ=aᵐaⁿ
(c) Generators are elements with order 8: 1, 3, 5, 7 (φ(8)=4)

questions.question: Let H and K be subgroups of G. Prove that HK is a subgroup of G if and only if HK = KH.
keyPoints: (⇒) If HK ≤ G, show HK = KH using inverses: (hk)⁻¹ = k⁻¹h⁻¹ ∈ KH
(⇐) If HK = KH, show HK is subgroup
HK is non-empty (contains e=e·e)
For closure: h₁k₁h₂k₂ = h₁(k₁h₂)k₂ = h₁h₂'k₂' ∈ HK using HK=KH
For inverses: (hk)⁻¹ = k⁻¹h⁻¹ ∈ KH = HK, hence inverse exists
Conclude HK ≤ G

questions.question: (a) State and prove Cauchy's Theorem for finite abelian groups. (b) If G is a group of order 12, show that G must contain an element of order 2 or 3.
keyPoints: (a) Cauchy: If p divides |G| for prime p, G has element of order p
Proof for abelian: use induction or consider cyclic subgroup
(b) By Cauchy, since 2|12 and 3|12, G has elements of orders 2 and 3
Alternatively, by Sylow theorems but Cauchy suffices
Thus G contains element of order 2 or 3
